Home/Epistemology/What is Knowledge?

Epistemology

What is Knowledge?

An Epistemology page on Knowledge, centered on belief, justification, uncertainty, and the standards by which claims become responsible.

  • Branch Epistemology
  • Page form Concept primer
  • Best for first orientation to a major concept
  • Difficulty Foundational to intermediate

Prompts

Prompts

  1. 1 We have already discussed the notion that “knowledge” is simply a degree of confidence that is subjectively determined. One person might consider a 95% level of confidence to be the threshold above which they consider something “knowledge,” and another person may consider an 80% level of confidence a sufficient threshold at which the credence is considered “knowledge.” Yet, some epistemologists treat knowledge as something that can be more objective, such as in the notion that “knowledge” is “justified, true belief.” Is this attempt to elevate the notion of “knowledge” to a more objective status justified when considering the conventional usage of “knowledge” that has much subjective variance in its definition and application? It appears some epistemologists are attempting to wrest the term “knowledge” away from its conventional usage and coerce the term into a stipulated definition. Is this correct?
  2. 2 Let’s take a closer look at the notion that “knowledge” is “justified, true belief”. This definition appears circular. Based on this definition, in order for someone to say they “know” something, the person must have assessed the knowledge as objectively true. However, this ability to objectively perceive something as true is not available to the subjectivity-bound person hoping to claim to “know” something. Once we take away the ability to use the term “know” in its most useful context in which we can subjectively claim we “know” it will rain tomorrow without needing access to the objective truth of the claim, then we have, indeed, wrest away the term from its conventional usage, and are providing a stipulation that has no relevance in daily life, right?
  3. 3 I suggest we abandon the highly problematic and tired project of finding a rigorous and coherent non-conventional definition of “knowledge”, and to instead focus on defining rational belief? Do you not think this would be a more successful focus, especially since the “epistemic turn” away from binary notions of belief and knowledge and towards more nuanced expressions of belief, such as credences and degrees of confidence?

Read This First

What gives this page its frame

If the page feels abrupt, these links supply the wider frame or earlier distinction that helps the present argument land.

Where the pressure naturally continues

    Direct expansion

    This page opens naturally into Types of Knowing, where one of its subquestions is treated more directly.

  2. ‘A Priori’ Knowledge Issues

    Direct expansion

    This page opens naturally into ‘A Priori’ Knowledge Issues, where one of its subquestions is treated more directly.

  3. Epistemology — Core Concepts

    Same branch

    Epistemology — Core Concepts keeps the same branch pressure in view but turns it from a different angle.

1

Composite Response

Prompt 1: We have already discussed the notion that “knowledge” is simply a degree of confidence that is subjectively determined. One person might consider a 95% level of confidence to be the threshold above which they consider something “knowledge,” and another person may consider an 80% level of confidence a sufficient threshold at which the credence is considered “knowledge.” Yet, some epistemologists treat knowledge as something that can be more objective, such as in the notion that “knowledge” is “justified, true belief.” Is this attempt to elevate the notion of “knowledge” to a more objective status justified when considering the conventional usage of “knowledge” that has much subjective variance in its definition and application? It appears some epistemologists are attempting to wrest the term “knowledge” away from its conventional usage and coerce the term into a stipulated definition. Is this correct?

Subjective Variance in Conventional Usage: practical stakes and consequences.

The section turns on Subjective Variance in Conventional Usage, Objective Status in Epistemology, and The Philosophical Project and Conventional Usage. Each piece is doing different work, and the page becomes thinner if the reader cannot say what is being identified, what is being tested, and what would change if one piece were removed.

In plain terms: The tension between the subjective variance in the definition and application of “knowledge” and the attempt by some epistemologists to give it a more objective status—such as defining knowledge as “justified, true belief”—highlights a fundamental challenge in epistemology.

The important discipline is to keep Subjective Variance in Conventional Usage distinct from Objective Status in Epistemology. They are not interchangeable bits of vocabulary; they direct the reader toward different judgments, objections, or next steps.

This opening move sets the terms for the rest of the page. It gives the reader a stable grip on the issue, so the later prompts can deepen it rather than circle around it.

This section should give the reader a usable epistemic lever: what would support the central claim, what would count against it, and what would make suspension of judgment more rational than either assent or denial. The point is not to make Knowledge tidy; it is to help the reader notice the difference between having a belief, having a reason, and having enough reason.

For a companion resource on calibration, credence, and structured rational judgment, see Credencing.com.

Truth

The proposition believed must be true.

Belief

The individual must believe the proposition.

Justification

The belief must be supported by adequate evidence or reasons.

Your question delves into a fundamental debate in epistemology

Is knowledge subjective or objective? You’re right to highlight the discrepancies between the everyday, subjective understanding of “knowledge” and the attempts of epistemologists to define it with more objective criteria.

Varying Certainty Thresholds

As you mentioned, individuals hold different levels of confidence to qualify something as “knowledge.” This subjectivity makes a universal definition challenging.

Cultural and Personal Influences

Individual experiences, cultural norms, and even personality traits can influence what someone considers “known.”

Innate vs. Acquired Knowledge

Some argue basic abilities like recognizing colors are innate and inherently known, while others may disagree, emphasizing the role of experience and evidence.

Justification Requirement

The “justified, true belief” definition aims to differentiate true belief (e.g., believing the Earth is round) from unjustified belief (e.g., believing the Earth is flat) to achieve objectivity.

Standardized Methods

Fields like science rely on shared methodologies and evidence-based approaches, fostering a collective agreement on what constitutes “knowledge” within specific domains.

Social Epistemology

This field highlights the role of communities and shared standards in establishing what counts as “knowledge” within certain contexts.

Epistemic Frameworks

Recognizing that different disciplines and contexts might require different levels of objectivity and rigor helps reconcile diverse understandings of “knowledge.”

Degrees of Knowledge

Instead of a binary “known/unknown” status, some propose a spectrum of confidence levels like “certain knowledge,” “justified belief,” and “uncertain belief.”

Language and Nuance

Using precise terms like “confidence,” “certainty,” and “justification” allows for more nuanced discussions about specific claims and the varying degrees of “knowing” in different contexts.

  1. Subjective Variance in Conventional Usage: In everyday discourse, the term “knowledge” indeed carries a degree of subjective variance.
  2. Objective Status in Epistemology: Epistemologists aim to analyze and clarify the concept of knowledge to address philosophical questions about its nature, sources, and limits.
  3. The Philosophical Project and Conventional Usage: The move to define knowledge more rigorously can be seen not as a coercive wresting away from conventional usage but as an attempt to refine our understanding of a critical concept.
  4. Reconciliation and Challenges: While the philosophical project seeks to elevate the notion of knowledge to a more objective status, it must also grapple with the realities of how knowledge is experienced and claimed in everyday life.
  5. Borderline case: The reader should be able to say what would make the claim merely plausible rather than justified.

What to notice

  • By the end, the reader should be able to say what difference Knowledge makes to judgment, not merely restate the issue in cleaner language.
  • The exchange works only if its movement through Truth, Belief, and Justification keeps bringing the issue into clearer focus. Each move should add pressure, clarity, or resistance, not just more length.
  • Track confidence calibration: what would raise, lower, or suspend belief without turning caution into paralysis?
2

Composite Response

Prompt 2: Let’s take a closer look at the notion that “knowledge” is “justified, true belief”. This definition appears circular. Based on this definition, in order for someone to say they “know” something, the person must have assessed the knowledge as objectively true. However, this ability to objectively perceive something as true is not available to the subjectivity-bound person hoping to claim to “know” something. Once we take away the ability to use the term “know” in its most useful context in which we can subjectively claim we “know” it will rain tomorrow without needing access to the objective truth of the claim, then we have, indeed, wrest away the term from its conventional usage, and are providing a stipulation that has no relevance in daily life, right?

Clear standards are what make Knowledge useful.

The section turns on The Challenge of Objective Truth, Subjectivity and Justification, and Practical Use of “Know”. Each piece is doing different work, and the page becomes thinner if the reader cannot say what is being identified, what is being tested, and what would change if one piece were removed.

In plain terms: The critique that the definition of knowledge as “justified, true belief” (JTB) appears circular or disconnects knowledge from its practical, everyday application raises important considerations about the relationship between philosophical definitions of knowledge and the.

The important discipline is to keep The Challenge of Objective Truth distinct from Subjectivity and Justification. They are not interchangeable bits of vocabulary; they direct the reader toward different judgments, objections, or next steps.

This middle step keeps the sequence honest. It takes the pressure already on the table and turns it toward the next distinction rather than letting the page break into separate mini-essays.

This section should give the reader a usable epistemic lever: what would support the central claim, what would count against it, and what would make suspension of judgment more rational than either assent or denial. The point is not to make Knowledge tidy; it is to help the reader notice the difference between having a belief, having a reason, and having enough reason.

Gettier Problems

Philosopher Edmund Gettier proposed scenarios where someone possesses justified true belief but isn’t said to have knowledge, highlighting the limitations of the JTB definition.

Subjectivity of Justification

What constitutes “justification” can be subjective and context-dependent, making the “objectivity” requirement difficult to fulfill.

Regress Problem

If justifying a belief requires another justified belief, how do we avoid an infinite regress of justifications without reaching a foundational base?

Defending JTB with Nuances

Some philosophers refine the definition by adding requirements like “the belief is not based on luck” or “the believer is aware of the justification,” addressing Gettier-type problems.

Alternative Definitions

Instead of JTB, some propose knowledge as warranted assertibility, socialized belief, or reliable belief formation processes.

Pragmatic Approach

While acknowledging the theoretical difficulties, accepting JTB as a useful framework for specific contexts like science or legal reasoning might be pragmatic.

Justification vs. Subjective Assessment

The justification component focuses on the evidential and rational basis for a belief. In contrast, the “subjectively assessed to be true” component acknowledges the epistemic agent’s role in interpreting and evaluating the evidence or reasons. While intertwined, they serve different purposes in the epistemological structure of knowledge.

Non-redundancy

The components are not redundant because justification provides the external grounding for a belief, whereas the subjective assessment addresses the internal conviction or acceptance of that belief’s truth by the individual. Both are necessary in the context of knowledge, but they emphasize different aspects of the epistemic process.

Objective Truth

The notion of truth in the JTB framework is objective in the sense that it is independent of individual feelings, desires, or interpretations. A statement or belief is considered true if it reflects the actual state of affairs.

Role in Knowledge

The requirement that a belief must be true to count as knowledge is foundational. It distinguishes knowledge from false beliefs or misconceptions, no matter how well-justified or sincerely held those beliefs might be.

Truth as Non-Negotiable

In this framework, the truth of a belief is non-negotiable for the belief to be considered knowledge. This sets a high standard, aiming to ensure that what we claim to know actually corresponds to reality.

Objective vs. Subjective

While the justification process and the belief component involve subjective elements (since they are mediated by human cognition and perception), the truth criterion aims to anchor knowledge claims in something beyond individual subjectivity.

Access to Objective Truth

One of the challenges in applying the JTB definition is determining how individuals can know with certainty that their beliefs correspond to objective truth. This has led to various philosophical debates and the development of additional criteria (e.g., the Gettier problem solutions) to strengthen the definition of knowledge.

Relativity and Constructivism

Some contemporary epistemological theories challenge the emphasis on objective truth, arguing for more relativistic or constructivist views of knowledge. These perspectives often highlight the roles of social, cultural, and historical factors in shaping what is considered “true.”

Examining the foundations

Everyday definitions are often intuitive but lack precise boundaries. Studying them helps us identify foundational assumptions and potential problems, leading to deeper understanding.

Exploring boundaries

Analyzing “knowledge” rigorously allows us to explore its limits and conditions, refining our understanding of what can be truly known and how.

Contextualizing different uses

Recognizing that language evolves and adapts to different contexts allows us to appreciate the nuances of both everyday and philosophical use of “knowledge,” enriching our overall understanding.

Explicitly stating the difference

Highlighting that the stipulated definition aims to analyze “knowledge” from a specific, rigorous perspective, distinct from everyday usage.

  1. The Challenge of Objective Truth: The requirement that knowledge must be based on what is “objectively true” presents a challenge, as it implies access to an infallible perspective or a means of verification beyond the reach of subjective human experience.
  2. Subjectivity and Justification: The JTB definition attempts to bridge subjective belief and objective truth by introducing “justification” as a critical component.
  3. Practical Use of “Know”: In everyday language, people frequently use “know” to express beliefs held with high confidence, even in the absence of absolute certainty or objective verification.
  4. Philosophical Rigor vs. Everyday Usage: The philosophical project of defining knowledge seeks to clarify and analyze the concept at a level of rigor that may not always align neatly with its everyday usage.
  5. Relevance and Application: While the JTB definition might seem removed from the subjective certainty of everyday claims of knowledge, it serves as a foundation for evaluating the reliability and validity of such claims.
  6. the curator’s Pushback: Asserting that knowledge must constitute “justified, true belief” (JTB) does indeed set a high standard for what counts as knowledge, one that emphasizes the alignment of belief with objective truth and adequate justification.

What to notice

  • By the end, the reader should be able to say what difference Knowledge makes to judgment, not merely restate the issue in cleaner language.
  • The exchange works only if its movement through Gettier Problems, Subjectivity of Justification, and Regress Problem keeps bringing the issue into clearer focus. Each move should add pressure, clarity, or resistance, not just more length.
  • Track confidence calibration: what would raise, lower, or suspend belief without turning caution into paralysis?
3

Composite Response

Prompt 3: I suggest we abandon the highly problematic and tired project of finding a rigorous and coherent non-conventional definition of “knowledge”, and to instead focus on defining rational belief? Do you not think this would be a more successful focus, especially since the “epistemic turn” away from binary notions of belief and knowledge and towards more nuanced expressions of belief, such as credences and degrees of confidence?

Acknowledgment of Complexity and Nuance: practical stakes and consequences.

The section turns on Acknowledgment of Complexity and Nuance, Pragmatic Value, and Epistemic Virtues. Each piece is doing different work, and the page becomes thinner if the reader cannot say what is being identified, what is being tested, and what would change if one piece were removed.

In plain terms: Shifting the focus from attempting to define “knowledge” in a non-conventional, rigorous manner to defining and understanding “rational belief” indeed represents a meaningful and potentially more fruitful direction in epistemology, particularly in light of the “epistemic turn”.

The important discipline is to keep Acknowledgment of Complexity and Nuance distinct from Pragmatic Value. They are not interchangeable bits of vocabulary; they direct the reader toward different judgments, objections, or next steps.

By the time the page reaches this final prompt, the clearing work should already be done. This last move should gather the earlier distinctions into a closing judgment rather than ending with a disconnected answer.

This section should give the reader a usable epistemic lever: what would support the central claim, what would count against it, and what would make suspension of judgment more rational than either assent or denial. The point is not to make Knowledge tidy; it is to help the reader notice the difference between having a belief, having a reason, and having enough reason.

Alignment with the “epistemic turn”

As you mentioned, the trend towards recognizing degrees of belief resonates with the concept of rational belief, which inherently involves expressing confidence levels and acknowledging uncertainty.

Avoidance of circular definitions

The “justified true belief” definition of knowledge can be circular, while rational belief can be defined based on specific criteria independent of “knowledge” itself.

Greater practical applicability

Understanding how to form, evaluate, and update beliefs rationally might be more directly applicable to decision-making and critical thinking in daily life.

Subjectivity of rationality

Standards for “rational” can vary depending on individual values, evidence evaluation methods, and cultural contexts.

Difficulties in quantification

While “degrees of confidence” offer a spectrum, precisely defining rational levels of belief for various situations can be complex.

Balance between rigor and flexibility

A rigid definition might be impractical, while too much flexibility could hinder clear communication and evaluation of beliefs.

Complementary perspectives

Analyzing “knowledge” can help identify its core characteristics and limitations, informing our understanding of rational belief formation and justification.

Co-evolving concepts

As we explore the nuances of “rational belief,” it might contribute to refining our understanding of “knowledge” and its relationship to certainty and justification.

  1. Acknowledgment of Complexity and Nuance: Focusing on rational belief acknowledges the complexity and nuance inherent in human cognition and belief formation.
  2. Pragmatic Value: Concentrating on rational belief has significant pragmatic value, especially in decision-making, critical thinking, and scientific inquiry.
  3. Epistemic Virtues: This shift encourages the cultivation of epistemic virtues such as open-mindedness, intellectual humility, and a commitment to evidence-based reasoning.
  4. Compatibility with the Epistemic Turn: The “epistemic turn” towards nuanced expressions of belief, including credences and degrees of confidence, reflects an evolving understanding of the nature of belief and knowledge.
  5. Addressing Skepticism and Relativism: A focus on rational belief can provide a robust framework for addressing challenges from skepticism and relativism.
  6. Interdisciplinary Relevance: Exploring the nature of rational belief has interdisciplinary relevance, connecting philosophical inquiry with cognitive science, psychology, sociology, and other fields.

What to notice

  • By the end, the reader should be able to say what difference Knowledge makes to judgment, not merely restate the issue in cleaner language.
  • The exchange works only if its movement through Alignment with the “epistemic turn”, Avoidance of circular definitions, and Greater practical applicability keeps bringing the issue into clearer focus. Each move should add pressure, clarity, or resistance, not just more length.
  • Track confidence calibration: what would raise, lower, or suspend belief without turning caution into paralysis?

Dialectical Turn

The exchange around Knowledge includes a real movement of judgment.

One pedagogical value of this page is that the prompts do not merely ask for more content. They sometimes force a model to retreat, concede, revise a category, or reframe the answer after the curator's pressure exposes a weakness.

That movement should be read as part of the argument. The important lesson is not simply that an AI changed its wording, but that a better prompt can make a prior stance answerable to logic, counterexample, or conceptual pressure.

  1. The curator's pushback is part of the argument, not a side note; it supplies the pressure that forces the response to become more exact.

What to notice

  • Track the turn in Knowledge: identify what the first answer missed and what pressure forced the later answer to change.
  • Do not treat concession as decoration; on this page, the revision is part of the argument about Knowledge.
  • Ask whether the later stance is genuinely stronger, or merely more cautious in tone.

Synthesis

The through-line is Subjective Variance in Conventional Usage, Objective Status in Epistemology, The Philosophical Project and Conventional Usage, and Reconciliation and Challenges.

The best route is to track how evidence changes credence, how justification differs from psychological comfort, and how skepticism can discipline thought without paralyzing it.

The recurring pressure is false certainty: treating a feeling of obviousness, a social consensus, or a useful assumption as if it had already earned the status of knowledge.

The section turns on Subjective Variance in Conventional Usage, Objective Status in Epistemology, and The Philosophical Project and Conventional Usage. Those pieces matter because they show what is being claimed, where it is tested, and what would change if the distinction actually holds.

Read this page as part of the wider Epistemology branch: the prompts point inward to the topic, but they also point outward to neighboring questions that keep the topic honest.

  1. Question 1: What traditional philosophical definition describes knowledge as?
  2. Question 2: According to the discussion, what is one key component that distinguishes knowledge from mere belief?
  3. Question 4: What philosophical problem questions the sufficiency of the “justified, true belief” definition of knowledge?
  4. Which distinction inside Knowledge is easiest to miss when the topic is explained too quickly?
  5. What is the strongest charitable reading of this topic, and what is the strongest criticism?

What to notice

  • By the end, the reader should be able to say what difference Knowledge makes to judgment, not merely restate the issue in cleaner language.
  • Use subjective Variance in Conventional Usage, Objective Status in Epistemology, and The Philosophical Project and Conventional Usage as the page's working clues. Each should narrow the field rather than merely decorate it.
  • Track confidence calibration: what would raise, lower, or suspend belief without turning caution into paralysis?
Q Deep Understanding Quiz Check your understanding of Knowledge +

This quiz checks whether the main distinctions and cautions on the page are clear. Choose an answer, read the feedback, and click the question text if you want to reset that item.

1

Correct. The page is not asking you merely to recognize Knowledge. It is asking what the idea does, what it explains, and where it needs limits.

Not quite. A definition can be useful, but this page is doing more than vocabulary work. It asks what distinctions make the idea usable.

Not quite. Speed is not the virtue here. The page trains slower judgment about what should be separated, connected, or held open.

Not quite. A pile of related ideas is not yet understanding. The useful work is seeing which ideas are central and where confusion enters.

2

Not quite. The details are not garnish. They are how the page teaches the main idea without flattening it.

Not quite. More terms do not help unless they sharpen a distinction, block a mistake, or clarify the pressure.

Not quite. Agreement is too cheap. The better test is whether you can explain why the distinction matters.

Correct. This part of the page is doing work. It gives the reader something to use, not just a heading to remember.

3

Not quite. General impressions can be useful starting points, but they are not enough here. The page asks the reader to track the actual distinctions.

Not quite. Familiarity can hide confusion. A reader can feel comfortable with a topic while still missing the structure that makes it important.

Correct. Many philosophical mistakes start by blending nearby ideas too early. Separate them first; then decide whether the connection is real.

Not quite. That may work casually, but the page is asking for more care. If two terms do different jobs, merging them weakens the argument.

4

Not quite. The uncomfortable parts are often where the learning happens. This page is trying to keep those tensions visible.

Correct. The harder question is this: The recurring pressure is false certainty: treating a feeling of obviousness, a social consensus, or a useful assumption as if it had already earned the status of knowledge. The quiz is testing whether you notice that pressure rather than retreating to the label.

Not quite. Complexity is not a reason to give up. It is a reason to use clearer distinctions and better examples.

Not quite. The branch name gives the page a home, but it does not explain the argument. The reader still has to see how the idea works.

5

Correct. That is stronger than remembering a definition. It shows you understand the claim, the objection, and the larger setting.

Not quite. Personal reaction matters, but it is not enough. Understanding requires explaining what the page is doing and why the issue matters.

Not quite. Definitions matter when they help us reason better. A repeated definition without a use is mostly verbal memory.

Not quite. Evaluation should come after charity. First make the view as clear and strong as the page allows; then judge it.

6

Not quite. That is usually a good move. Strong objections help reveal whether the argument has real strength or only surface appeal.

Not quite. That is part of good reading. The archive depends on connection without careless merging.

Not quite. Qualification is not a failure. It is often what keeps philosophical writing honest.

Correct. This is the shortcut the page resists. A familiar word can feel clear while still hiding the real philosophical issue.

7

Not quite. The structure exists to support the argument. It should help the reader see relationships, not replace understanding.

Not quite. A good branch does not postpone clarity. It gives the reader a way to carry clarity into the next question.

Correct. Here, useful next steps include Types of Knowing and ‘A Priori’ Knowledge Issues. The links are not decoration; they show where the pressure continues.

Not quite. Links matter only when they help the reader think. Empty branching would make the archive busier but not wiser.

8

Not quite. A slogan may be memorable, but understanding requires seeing the moving parts behind it.

Correct. This treats the synthesis as a tool for further thinking, not just a closing paragraph. In the page's own terms, The best route is to track how evidence changes credence, how justification differs from psychological comfort, and how.

Not quite. A synthesis should gather what has been learned. It is not just a polite way to stop talking.

Not quite. Philosophical work often makes disagreement sharper and more responsible. It rarely makes all disagreement disappear.

Future Branches

Where this page naturally expands

Epistemology Primer Knowledge Belief Credence Inquiry Language

This branch opens directly into Types of Knowing and ‘A Priori’ Knowledge Issues, so the reader can move from the present argument into the next natural layer rather than treating the page as a dead end. Nearby pages in the same branch include Epistemology — Core Concepts, What is Epistemology?, Core & Deep Rationality, and What is Belief?; those links are not decorative, but suggested continuations where the pressure of this page becomes sharper, stranger, or more usefully contested.
